reasoning:
Verdict: REJECT (34% confidence)

Module Scores:
  Sentinel (CWE/OWASP Web Application Security): 50/100 [Low risk] — 1 info
  Watchdog (OWASP LLM Top 10 / Cisco AI Threat Taxonomy): 99/100 [Info risk] — 1 info
  Guardian (NIST AI RMF / EU AI Act / UNICC Responsible AI): 44/100 [High risk] — 6 high, 3 critical, 5 medium, 1 low

Arbitration Triggers:
  • REJECT trigger — critical findings in Guardian: [guardian-3] No explicit EU AI Act risk classification or fundamental rights impact assessment for autonomous media verification; [guardian-4] Lack of any bias or fairness testing for media authenticity and moderation decisions; [guardian-8] No human oversight, review workflow, or override mechanisms for automated moderation

Disagreement Resolution:
  ⚠ Watchdog scored 99/100 (info risk) while Sentinel scored 50/100 (low risk) — Δ49 point divergence. Arbitration defers to the stricter assessment from Sentinel to maintain safety margins.
  ⚠ Risk-level conflict: Guardian rates high risk while Sentinel rates low risk on the same application. Council defers to Guardian's stricter assessment.
  ⚠ Watchdog scored 99/100 (info risk) while Guardian scored 44/100 (high risk) — Δ55 point divergence. Arbitration defers to the stricter assessment from Guardian to maintain safety margins.

Confidence Factors:
  • -30% confidence: 3 disagreement(s) between modules
  • Base score: 64/100 average across 3 module(s)

--- LLM-Enhanced Council Narrative ---
Across the three assessments, there is strong agreement that the evidentiary base for VeriMedia AI is extremely weak: Sentinel and Watchdog both report that no meaningful source code or configuration artifacts are available, while Guardian notes the repository is effectively empty of governance and architecture documentation. This alignment reinforces that any numeric scoring should be treated cautiously, as all modules are largely inferring from a high-level system description rather than verifiable implementation details. At the same time, Guardian layers on a rich set of governance, rights, and regulatory concerns—bias and fairness, privacy, EU AI Act high-risk classification, lack of human oversight, and missing auditability—painting a picture of a functionally ambitious system with almost no documented controls.

There are important conflicts in how residual risk is summarized. Sentinel assigns a mid-range score and labels web/application risk as low despite explicitly stating it could not perform a real assessment, while Watchdog gives an almost perfect score and only informational risk level because no LLM usage is visible. These optimistic ratings clash with Guardian’s high-risk posture and critical findings on autonomous, public-facing moderation decisions without safeguards. The net effect is an underestimation of technical and AI-specific risk by Sentinel and Watchdog when evidence is absent, versus Guardian’s more cautious, impact-focused stance. Additional gaps remain, including limited consideration of ML model and deployment supply-chain security and a lack of explicit treatment of how any future LLM-based explanation or moderation features would intersect with the serious governance deficiencies already identified.

top_findings: Insufficient application source code for security analysis
sentinel.summary: VeriMedia AI, a Flask/Python-based media verification platform, could not be assessed because no source code, configuration, or dependency information was provided. As a result, no concrete security weaknesses or strengths in its web application and API layers could be verified.
sentinel.recommendation: 1) Collect and provide the full Flask/Python backend source tree and configuration files for targeted security review. 2) Include dependency manifests (requirements.txt/pyproject.toml) to enable dependency vulnerability analysis. 3) Supply deployment-related configs (e.g., nginx, AWS infrastructure-as-code) to assess transport security and exposure of public-facing endpoints.

top_findings: No detectable LLM or tool-calling integration in VeriMedia AI codebase
watchdog.summary: VeriMedia AI is described as a Flask-based media verification platform using conventional deep learning models (ResNet for vision, BERT for NLP) and automated moderation logic. No explicit Large Language Model technologies (e.g., GPT-4o, Claude, Llama, LangChain, tool-calling frameworks, or RAG systems) or corresponding source code were provided. As a result, no concrete LLM-specific risks (prompt injection, insecure tool design, excessive agency, etc.) can be tied to actual code paths, files, o
watchdog.recommendation: 1) If VeriMedia AI actually integrates any LLMs (for example, using OpenAI, Anthropic, or open-source LLMs for explanation, summarisation, or fact-checking), provide the relevant source code (Flask routes, background workers, agent definitions) so that prompt usage, tool access, and output handling can be evaluated against OWASP-LLM01–10. 2) Before introducing LLM-based features (e.g., LLM-driven fact-check explanations or moderation reasoning), design prompts and tool interfaces with strong sep

top_findings: Complete absence of governance and architecture documentation for VeriMedia AI
No model cards or documentation for ResNet and BERT models used in media verification
No explicit EU AI Act risk classification or fundamental rights impact assessment for autonomous media verification
guardian.summary: VeriMedia AI is an autonomous media verification and content moderation platform using ResNet and BERT with public-facing APIs but an effectively empty repository lacking governance, documentation, and risk controls. Core gaps include missing model cards, absence of bias and fairness evaluation, no privacy and data governance documentation, and no human oversight or auditability structures despite high potential impact on public discourse and fundamental rights. guardian.recommendation:
